findings.summary: Four kickoff memos attributed to four different advisors are one templated hand, down to a first-person aphorism reproduced word for word across two of them.
findings.evidence: d:0003 (Daniel Valenzuela) and d:0020 (Michael Morris) each contain, verbatim, 'A problem raised on the day it appears is a scheduling note; the same problem raised a week later is an apology.' All four open the same paragraph with 'the value of a [positioning engagement / investor relations program / audit] leaks out through small inconsistencies [/ sloppiness] more than through any single misstep [/ wrong call]' and close with 'Keep the client-facing channel ... anything going to [contact] or anyone else at [client] comes through me.' A first-person memo about 'how I want this one run' is not a circulated template; two different named authors do not independently write the identical maxim.

findings.summary: The 'I/we would rather X than Y' antithesis is a single writer's reflex worn by every persona, including the ones the roster casts as brisk, dry, or logistics-only, so it reads as one hand rather than a house style.
findings.evidence: Managing partner Julie Hill: 'we would rather miss a news cycle than put out a sentence that has not been approved' (d:0032). Dry, evidence-first analyst Kimberly Le: 'I would rather choose between real options than talk myself into one early' (d:0084). Brisk, unfussy associate John Macias: 'I would rather she land it right than land it fast' (d:0068). Michelle Rose: 'I would rather ask him once than twice' (d:0081). The construction appears 36 times in the sampled prose alone; GENERATION-REPORT pre-registers the antithesis pattern and counts it (32 loose, 106 plain across 23/49 docs) but renders no verdict. The verdict: the reflex is uniform where the personas predict it should not be, which is the mark of one author, not a firm.

findings.summary: Nearly every author lands a paragraph on a polished antithetical maxim, a flourish uniform across personas who are cast as not writing that way.
findings.evidence: A mundane holiday-coverage internal note by Christopher Walton closes 'a client waiting three days on a reply they think is urgent is what turns a quiet weekend loud' (d:0057). The dry analyst Kimberly Le: 'the kind of gap that reads as evasiveness from the outside even when it is only disorganization on the inside' (d:0078). Nancy Suarez, in minutes: 'a page people actually read beats a binder they file' (d:0044). John Macias: 'Late notice is the expensive kind.' (d:0063). The epigrammatic closer is the same instrument in every hand, from a logistics email to an analyst's note, which no real fifteen-person bench would share.

findings.summary: Meeting minutes and status reports by different authors share identical section openers that the same-genre similarity metric did not surface.
findings.evidence: Minutes open 'The session took stock of the [work/program] at [its] midpoint' in d:0014 (Michelle Rose), d:0023 (Michelle Black), and d:0055 (Melissa Zimmerman). Status-report risk sections open on a two-item list whose second pole is always the same: 'Two things are worth watching. The first is ... The second is timing.' in d:0024 (Michael Morris), and 'Two risks are worth naming plainly. The first is ... The second is timing.' in d:0042 (Amy Carter) and d:0056 (Laura Bradley). The same-genre Jaccard table flagged only engagement letters and one kickoff pair, so these cross-author structural clones are exactly what the metric missed and the board is for.

findings.summary: The engagement emails, the firm's most persona-driven writing, share a cloned request-and-schedule scaffold across authors.
findings.evidence: 'Send me a couple of windows that work ... and I will set it up' recurs near-verbatim in d:0010 (Daniel Valenzuela), d:0021 (Michael Morris), and d:0040 (Amy Carter). Each pairs it with the same body-opening ask ('The one thing that would help now', 'The one ask for now', 'Two things would help before we go further', 'What would help most right now', 'Two things I need from you before we go further') in d:0004/d:0021/d:0040/d:0047/d:0064. The scaffolding is authored body prose, not the appended signature or quoted tail, and it is the same across advisors the charter says 'write plainly and quickly' in their own voices.
